All reports
REDX

Competitive Intelligence Report

The battlefield map: where REDX wins head-to-head, where rivals are exposed, and the lines a rep uses to close against each one.

Prepared by [Author - to be filled]
June 2026

0. Executive Summary

Bottom line. REDX competes on three fronts at once and the strongest one is the cheapest to win. Against the data-and-dialer rivals (Vulcan7, Mojo, Landvoice, Espresso Agent) REDX ties on data and wins on system breadth and price, but only after it fixes the accuracy complaints its own customers file alongside theirs. Against the wallet-share players (Zillow, BombBomb) REDX wins on lead intent and predictable cost, because a Zillow lead converting at 1-3% is a worse deal than an expired-listing owner who must transact1. The single biggest exposure is shared: every vendor in this category is sued on data accuracy, so a clean, honest data experience is the lane no rival has earned.

REDX wins the head-to-head on channel breadth and on integrated coaching tied to published proof, and it ties on raw data quality. Vulcan7, Mojo, Landvoice, and Espresso Agent are all phone-first or data-only; none pairs a multi-channel prospecting system with a published library of named-agent outcomes234. REDX’s case-study wall carries 16 named agents with hard numbers, including $14M in volume, $522K, $300K, and 160-plus FSBOs5678. No rival publishes a proof library of that depth.

REDX ties, and cannot yet win, on data. Its own G2 and BBB reviews carry the same accuracy complaints that sink Mojo and Landvoice: disconnected numbers, DNC-flagged numbers sold as callable, expireds already relisted91011. Data accuracy is the most dominant negative review theme across the entire category, REDX included12. The “best data” claim is unwinnable until the hygiene problem is fixed.

The single biggest open lane is a clean data experience honestly described. Vulcan7 contradicts its own data-superiority numbers on two live pages, 14% against 20%13. Landvoice’s “no long-term contracts” marketing is contradicted by its own BBB refund-denial response14. Espresso ships a site with literal “0 Agents Served” counters and a competitor’s template labels still in the navigation1516. The category’s credibility floor is low. A vendor that publishes real prices, honest accuracy expectations, and a genuine cancellation policy owns trust ground no incumbent currently holds.

The single biggest exposure REDX must manage is its own version of the category data problem. Every defense line below depends on REDX not being the worst offender on the one complaint every buyer in this market has already heard.

1. Competitor Matrix and Client Snapshot

The buyer is a real-estate agent choosing where to spend a prospecting budget. The dimensions that decide that choice are data scope, whether there is a real dialer, channel breadth beyond the phone, whether coaching is included, price posture, and contract honesty.

Figure 2. Capability presence per competitor. Pull this up on a sales call when a prospect asks “how do you compare to X?” - REDX is the only column that fills system breadth, coaching, named-agent proof, and a low published price together.

Dimension REDX Vulcan7 Mojo Landvoice Espresso Agent
Core data Expired, FSBO, FRBO, pre-foreclosure, GeoLeads Expired, FSBO, FRBO, pre-foreclosure, geo Expired, FSBO, FRBO, pre-foreclosure, neighborhood Expired, FSBO, pre-foreclosure, neighborhood Expired, FSBO, FRBO, pre-foreclosure, geo
Outbound dialer Yes (Power Dialer) Yes (single-line) Yes (single/triple, 300 calls/hr)17 No (inbound Call Capture only)4 Yes (single-line, manual only)3
Channels beyond phone Multi-channel (ads, mail, video, text/email follow-up) Phone-first; StoryTellr video email2 Phone-first; thin AI multi-touch add-on18 Data-only; Inked Cards direct mail19 Phone-only by design3
Coaching / community Yes (guided) Yes (Take 52, twice-weekly Zoom)20 None21 None Yes (Launch, Objection Slayers)22
Published pricing Yes No prices shown23 Yes, a-la-carte17 Yes24 Yes25
Entry price ~$59/mo24 ~$359/mo all-in26 $149/mo min before data27 $87-$197/mo bundles24 $199-$399/mo25
Contract honesty Customer complaints exist9 Markets “cancel anytime”; reviews report pushed 12-month no-refund28 “No contracts” but billing complaints9 “No long-term contracts” contradicted by own BBB response14 Month-to-month; 15-day notice on term plans25

REDX snapshot. REDX sells outbound seller-prospecting at roughly $59 entry, the lowest published floor in the direct set24. It is the only rival that pairs a real outbound dialer, multi-channel follow-up (ads, mail, video, text, email), and an included coaching layer with a public library of 16 named-agent case studies512. Its visible strength is that combination plus price. Its visible weakness is data accuracy: REDX customers file the same disconnected-number and DNC complaints that sink its rivals91029. The claimed differentiator REDX can defend in the market is parity-grade data inside the most complete system, at roughly half a premium rival’s price, with the coaching and proof that turn a willing agent’s effort into listings.

2. Per-Competitor Deep Dives

2.1 Vulcan7: the premium incumbent whose premium is reputational

  1. Primary surface and positioning. vulcan7.com leads with “Real Estate Prospecting That Turns Leads into Listings” and an eyebrow reading “#1 Real Estate Platform,” self-described as an “All-In-One Prospecting Platform” built for outbound seller “Hunters.” The hero stat band claims “#1 contact data accuracy,” “dial up to 4x faster,” and “15+ years as the industry standard.”
  2. Real posture versus claimed. Vulcan7’s only candidate moat is a data-spend claim, “invests up to 11x more on data than competitors,” paired with a premium brand reputation that reviewers and coaches reinforce as the “Bentley” or “Ferrari” of real-estate data3031. That brand premium rests on perception. It collapses the moment a rival demonstrates data parity31. A spend advantage is copyable by any capitalized competitor including REDX. The 12-month no-refund contract that third-party reviews describe is a manufactured switching cost that punishes the buyer for leaving28. Vulcan7 holds a soft Branding advantage and a contestable data-spend claim. It holds no network effect, no decisive scale lead, and no counter-positioning.
  3. Target and scope. Full-time outbound seller-prospecting agents working expired, FSBO, FRBO, and pre-foreclosure leads, explicitly not inbound buyer-lead seekers. Seats and whitelisted numbers tier up from one to three; lower-cost data types are add-ons. US and Canada.
  • Claimed differentiators, verbatim. “Vulcan7 invests up to 11x more on data than competitors”; “14% more unique numbers per listing than any other provider”; “#1 contact data accuracy”; “Up to 4x Faster Dialing”30.
  1. Visible strengths. Fifteen-plus years and an entrenched premium reputation; named testimonials with real affiliations; a 4.7-star Birdeye aggregate over 393 reviews; an all-in-one bundle of data, single-line power dialer, CRM, video email, and training including “Take 52 Listings” and twice-weekly live Zoom sessions positioned as “Never be alone”2032.
  2. Visible weaknesses. No prices on the public packages page, forcing a sales call23. The most expensive option in its class at roughly $359/month26. A contract trap that contradicts its own “cancel anytime” copy28. An interface reviewers compare to “Windows 95.” A closed ecosystem charging a $25-50/month middleware tax for Follow Up Boss or kvCORE sync. Data-accuracy complaints against the very platform whose premium is data quality: one customer paying more than $900 over three months reported “the answer is 0… the wrong number or that person already bought the home”33. A phone-first product with no native multi-channel orchestration2.
  3. Recent dated activity. A 2026 site refresh with new hero video and headshots uploaded May 2026; an active 2025-2026 blog cadence; and a new “ChatGPT’s #1 recommended realtor prospecting tool” banner that links to a ChatGPT share URL as its cited proof. No confirmed funding, acquisition, or leadership change was found.
  4. Overlap with REDX. Near-total. Both sell expired and FSBO data with a dialer, both lean on coach and testimonial social proof, both push training, and both make near-identical “best data” claims, which turns the data-parity argument into a direct collision rather than a differentiated lane.
  5. The open lane next to Vulcan7. Vulcan7 hides its prices and locks buyers into a 12-month no-refund term while marketing the opposite2328. REDX publishes a ~$59 entry price and a genuine monthly option24. The rep’s wedge is transparency against a $359 black-box subscription: same-grade data, multi-channel reach Vulcan7’s single-line dialer cannot match, a published price, and no contract trap. Reframe the premium reputation as a brand tax the buyer is paying for fifteen-year-old positioning, not better outcomes.

2.2 Mojo Sells: the dialer that is losing on data and on its own call-quality claim

Mojo’s identity is the phone. Its homepage H1 is “Mojo Dialer: Accelerate Your Calling and Maximize Conversations,” and its power-dialer page brands it “The Call Quality Leader” promising up to 300 calls per hour34. The dialer is the hero; data and CRM are supporting add-ons.

  1. Real posture versus claimed. Mojo’s one defensible edge is a genuine technical claim: a dedicated, copper-based, non-VoIP telecom path that competitors cannot trivially replicate35. That is a narrow Cornered Resource around call quality, plus modest Branding from a 2007-vintage name. Everything else is weak. Switching costs are low because there are no contracts. There is no data moat, no scale economy, and no network effect.
  2. Target and scope. Real-estate agents and investors first, with insurance, mortgage, and inside-sales teams named as secondary verticals; solo agents up to enterprise teams of 100-plus. Data products are US-only.
  3. Pricing and contract. A transparent a-la-carte “build your plan” model with no contracts: Agent Access $10/user/month (required), Single Line Dialer $89, Triple Line Dialer $139, plus separately priced data (Expired $50, FSBO $25, FRBO $25, Pre-Foreclosure $49, Neighborhood Search $49, Skip Tracer $49)17. The low headline hides the real number: a single agent on Triple Line plus Agent Access is a $149/month minimum before any data, and realistic configurations run “north of $250/user/month”27.
  • Claimed differentiators, verbatim. The copper-based call-quality pitch35; “make up to 300 calls per hour”; a “Reputation Guard” with STIR/SHAKEN Attestation A certification36; and an “AI Action Plan Builder” for multi-touch campaigns “that combine calls, letters, and drip emails”18.
  1. Visible strengths. Genuine high-volume throughput, a real call-quality differentiator versus VoIP rivals, a current spam-mitigation stack, flexible no-contract pricing, and a recognized brand in the dialer category36.
  2. Visible weaknesses. Data quality is the dominant external knock: Capterra reviewers call the data “extremely inaccurate,” and aggregations cite agents reaching “2 people out of 300 phone calls” and “99% of numbers old or disconnected”37. Modular pricing inflates the real cost27. Billing and cancellation complaints recur9. There is no coaching or academy layer at all21. And the call-quality hero claim is undercut by a Trustpilot “Poor” 2.3/5 with complaints of “terrible audio quality” and a dialer that “hangs up on customers mid sentence”38.
  3. Recent dated activity. Reputation Guard and Caller ID whitelisting rolled out through 2025-2026; AI calling scripts and the Action Plan Builder added to the homepage; the pricing page last modified October 14, 2025 and the about page November 10, 2025; 2026 blog content pushing door-knocking and email adjacencies.
  4. Overlap with REDX. Both sell the same expired-FSBO-FRBO-pre-foreclosure data plus a dialer, a near-identical SKU shape, and agents frequently compare the two on cost.
  5. The open lane next to Mojo. Mojo coaches no one. Its site carries no academy, no community, no guided onboarding, only a support team it describes as “Built by Sales Professionals, for Sales Professionals”21. REDX’s guided coaching and proof library sit on ground Mojo does not contest. Layer the data-quality wedge on top: Mojo’s loudest complaint is dead data, and a rep can hand a Mojo refugee a cleaner list plus the coaching Mojo never offered.

2.3 Landvoice: the curated-data house whose cancellation reputation is its soft flank

Landvoice runs a pure seller-data play. Its homepage H1 is “Homeowners Who Need to Sell Now,” and it claims “over 30 years” of “putting more For Sale signs in yards than any other provider”39. It makes no claim to be an all-in-one platform.

  1. Real posture versus claimed. Landvoice’s genuine asset is a curated-data philosophy: Expired Pro reaches “up to 95%” of numbers from “elite databases,” and the company gives only three ranked numbers rather than every number found40. It explicitly counter-positions against REDX’s volume model on its own pricing page, with the rare credibility of a CEO who is one of REDX’s original founders4142. That counter-position is a genuine philosophical fork between curated quality and contact volume, and it gives Landvoice a marketing stance rather than a structural moat. With no dialer and no CRM lock-in, Landvoice carries the lowest switching cost in the set, which is a structural weakness against any platform player4.
  2. Target and scope. US and Canada agents and teams, tiered by experience from budget-starter to “nearly every number” Pro. Canada gets FSBO data only.
  3. Pricing and contract. Bundles run $87/month (Starter) to $197/month (Elite promo, regularly $227); a-la-carte starts at FSBO $40/month and Expired Pro $120/month24. Marketing brags “no long-term contracts” and mocks competitors’ setup fees43.
  • Claimed differentiators, verbatim. A direct attack on REDX from the pricing FAQ: the REDX CEO “is on record saying they provide every number they can find to create opportunities. So, you end up getting many numbers to people that have no relation or tie to the property”41. Landvoice instead gives “only 3 numbers” ranked by likelihood, plus “For Sale By Owner listings from 1,700+ sources daily” and DNC indication41.
  1. Visible strengths. A defensible quality-first counter-narrative, no setup fee, a low $40 entry, long tenure with founder credibility, and new tooling in Data Genie (contact enrichment) and Inked Cards (handwritten direct mail)19.
  2. Visible weaknesses. No outbound dialer, only inbound Call Capture4. Data-only means low switching cost. A refund and cancellation complaint pattern that drags its BBB customer review average to 1/51444. And the curated “3 numbers only” model leaves high-activity prospectors short on contact volume.
  3. Recent dated activity. Inked Cards launched as “All New” in the 2025-26 product navigation; Data Genie added as a recent contact-enrichment tool; Distressed (financial-hardship homeowner data) added; active 2026-dated blog content.
  4. Overlap with REDX. Direct head-to-head on core seller data, a shared origin through Landvoice’s founder, a comparable price band, and DNC scrubbing on both sides.
  5. The open lane next to Landvoice. Landvoice tells the market it has “no long-term contracts,” then tells a BBB reviewer “our policy does not allow for refunds after the commitment is made”4314. The gap between the promise and the billing is the rep’s opening. REDX’s wedge is the dialer Landvoice does not have plus honest billing the buyer can verify: when a Landvoice customer says they were locked into a year and refused a refund, the rep offers a published monthly price and the integrated outbound dialer that turns a list into conversations.

2.4 Espresso Agent: the affordable feature-clone with a credibility-leaking website

Espresso Agent leads with “Dominate Your Market with Affordable Real Estate Listing Leads” and the tagline “Confidence Starts Here.” Affordability against pricier rivals is the whole positioning.

Real posture versus claimed. Espresso’s only defensible power is counter-positioning on price: an all-in-one bundle that undercuts Vulcan7 while including CRM and dialer, reinforced by soft switching costs from its coaching community2522. It claims “industry-leading” scale, but its own About page renders the proof as literal zeros (see below)15. Its data-superiority claim hedges with “where available”45. There is no exclusive data source and no scale or network moat.

  • The ownership question, settled against the record. REDX leadership believes Espresso Agent is a Vulcan7 sub-brand. Public evidence contradicts that. Espresso is operated by The Symphony Source, LLC at 195 W Main Street, American Fork, UT, per its pricing-page consent text and its subscription agreement46. Its About page states it was launched in 2014 by “a group of seasoned real estate agents,” with no mention of Vulcan747. Vulcan7 is a separate company headquartered in Cincinnati, Ohio and founded around 2010, a different state, year, and entity48. Espresso is a near feature-clone of Vulcan7, which likely seeded the rumor, but feature parity is not ownership. Treat the two as independent direct competitors. If REDX holds private knowledge of common ownership, that is non-public and must be confirmed before it is ever stated.

Target and scope. The same cold-calling listing-agent ICP as REDX: expired, FSBO, FRBO, pre-foreclosure, and geo data. Self-serve signup; US plus a Canada-only FSBO SKU.

Pricing and contract. Basic $199/month, Pro $279/month (“Most Popular”), Platinum $399/month, plus a $149/month Canada FSBO-only plan25. Marketed month-to-month, with longer 6/12/24-month options requiring 15 days’ cancellation notice before auto-renewal25. Every package bundles CRM, single-line dialer, video email, and whitelisted numbers49.

  • Claimed differentiators, verbatim. “unmatched, real-time contact information, including multiple phone numbers (with cell numbers) and verified email addresses where available”; “The Leader in Neighborhood Search Prospecting”; a single dialer “as effective as a triple dialer”45.
  1. Visible strengths. A price-competitive all-in-one bundle, a strong data-accuracy and customer-service sentiment in reviews, a heavy coaching community (Launch, Objection Slayers, twice-weekly masterminds), a modern AI-equipped dialer, and a low-friction month-to-month entry2245.
  2. Visible weaknesses. Phone-only by design and proud of it: its own dialer page states “One call at a time,” “No automation, no robo-dialing,” and “No auto-texting or pre-recorded voicemails”3. A site hygiene problem detailed in Section 7. A hedged data claim. A smaller brand and scale than Vulcan7 or REDX.
  3. Recent dated activity. AI dialer features (background-noise suppression, call transcription and summaries) live and marketed as the 2026 product emphasis; coaching tribes and masterminds ongoing. No dated press release, funding round, acquisition, or executive change was found.
  4. Overlap with REDX. The most feature-parallel direct rival in scope: identical lead types, dialer, CRM, coaching, and overlapping price bands.
  5. The open lane next to Espresso. Espresso refuses SMS automation and voicemail drops on principle and ships a website carrying a competitor’s template labels and broken counters31615. REDX’s wedge is channel breadth and polish: a compliant multi-channel cadence (call, text, email, video) against a tool that markets one call at a time, sold by a vendor whose own pages say “0 Agents Served.”

3. Whitespace Map

Figure 1. Each dot is a competitor; dot size reflects market presence. The faint rust-shaded quadrant - multi-channel breadth paired with coaching proven by named agents - is the uncontested lane only REDX occupies.

An apparently open lane carries a reason a competitor has left it, which a rep must respect.

Honest data experience (genuinely open). No vendor in this category has earned trust on data accuracy. The complaint is universal, REDX included12. The lane opens only if REDX fixes its own hygiene first; until then it is open ground REDX cannot yet occupy. This is the report’s central tension.

Price transparency with genuine flexibility (genuinely open). Vulcan7 hides prices; Landvoice and Mojo carry billing-trust complaints23149. A published price plus a real monthly cancellation, verifiable by the buyer, is a lane no incumbent holds cleanly.

Multi-channel orchestration (genuinely open against the direct set). Vulcan7, Mojo, Landvoice, and Espresso are phone-first or data-only21843. A compliant integrated cadence across call, text, email, video, and ads is uncontested among the direct rivals.

Coaching tied to a published proof library (genuinely open). Vulcan7 and Espresso both coach, so generic coaching is table stakes2022. What no rival publishes is REDX’s depth of named-agent outcomes: 16 case studies with hard numbers5. The defensible lane is coaching proven by named peers who put up real listing numbers.

The new and part-time agent who will work but is priced out of the team stack (apparently open). A solo agent’s modern tooling runs $2,000-plus per month, and over 80% of new agents leave before their first renewal, largely from lack of prospecting support50. The lane sits between cheap raw-data vendors and expensive team platforms. It is only apparently open because serving it well requires the guided coaching and onboarding that raw-data rivals deliberately skip to stay cheap. REDX can take it only by leading with the system-plus-coaching identity and treating the data as the on-ramp.

Seller-lead intent against the portal model (genuinely open on the wallet-share front). Zillow’s buyer leads convert at 1-3% and are sold to multiple agents151. Must-transact seller leads are a categorically different prospect, and no portal contests that ground.

4. TCO Comparison Logic

Sticker price misleads in this category because the real number depends on seat floors, required add-ons, data subscriptions, and contract minimums. The figures below are individually sourced; there is no aggregate comparison table because no honest one can be built from published terms alone.

  1. Vulcan7. No price is published; the buyer must call sales23. Third-party reporting puts the all-in bundle at roughly $359/month, about $4,308/year, with an a-la-carte option near $25026. The hidden cost is the contract: reviews describe a pushed 12-month no-refund term, so the effective commitment is a year even when the buyer believed it was monthly28. Add a $25-50/month middleware tax for two-way CRM sync.
  2. Mojo. The headline looks cheap and is not. Agent Access at $10 is required, the Triple Line Dialer is $139, so the floor is $149/month before a single lead17. Add Expired ($50), FSBO ($25), and Skip Tracer ($49) and a realistic single-agent config clears $250/month27.
  3. Landvoice. Bundles run $87-$197/month, a-la-carte from $40 (FSBO) to $120 (Expired Pro)24. The unpriced cost is the annual-billing trap that BBB reviewers report, where a monthly impression becomes a year’s commitment with refunds denied14.
  4. Espresso Agent. $199 (Basic) to $399 (Platinum) monthly, with Pro at $27925. The term-plan discount carries a 15-day-before-renewal cancellation notice; miss it and the term auto-renews25.
  5. REDX. Published entry around $59/month, the lowest visible floor, with data, dialer, and follow-up tools stacking from there24. The TCO argument for REDX is that its floor is transparent and its commitment honest, which is exactly the comparison the rep should force: published monthly price against a $359 black box or a $250 modular stack.

5. Migration Friction Map and Anti-Switching Triggers

Switching cost and switching trigger are different things. The cost is what leaving an incumbent takes; the trigger is the moment a buyer starts looking. Below, each incumbent’s switch cost is named, then the listener cues a rep should hear, each drawn from a real customer-review verbatim.

Vulcan7. Switch cost is high by design: a 12-month no-refund contract plus a closed CRM ecosystem28. The trigger is almost always price shock or accuracy disappointment that the contract then traps. Listener cues:

  • “I’m paying more than $900 and the answer is 0” (the customer is getting no listings from a premium they cannot escape)33.
  • “The wrong number or that person already bought the home” (the data does not match the premium)33.
  • “I pay for the annual membership” (the buyer is locked and may be looking for an exit)52.

Mojo. Switch cost is low; there are no contracts. The triggers are dead data and call-quality glitches. Listener cues:

  • “The data they provide is extremely inaccurate”37.
  • “It hangs up on customers mid sentence” (the call-quality claim is failing them)38.
  • “Got billed after cancelling” (billing trust has broken)9.

Landvoice. Switch cost is low (data-only), but the auto-renewal billing can trap a buyer who thought they were month-to-month. Triggers cluster on refund disputes and stale data. Listener cues:

  • “They charged me again and will not refund my money”44.
  • “Consistently late with their data or it is entirely incorrect” (the curated-quality promise is failing)53.
  • “The people I reached had not listed their homes in years, making me look unprofessional”53.

Espresso Agent. Switch cost is moderate via CRM data and community ties; the term plans carry a notice-window trap. The triggers are channel limits and the polish gap. Listener cues:

  • “I can only make one call at a time” (the buyer has hit the phone-only ceiling)3.
  • “I want to text and email these leads too, not just call” (channel demand Espresso refuses by design)3.

6. Compliance Posture

The category runs on phone outreach, so telecom regulation is the operating frame. Two forces matter and they point in opposite directions.

  1. The federal consent rule loosened. The FCC’s TCPA “one-to-one consent” rule, which would have required consent for one specific named seller before autodialing or texting, was vacated by the Eleventh Circuit in Insurance Marketing Coalition v. FCC on January 24, 2025, three days before its effective date; in April 2025 the FCC declined to challenge the ruling54. The federal consent requirement on cold outreach loosened.
  2. Litigation risk intensified anyway. TCPA filings hit 2,788 in 2024, up 67% over 2023 and the highest on record, with about 80% filed as class actions; class actions rose 95.2% through mid-2025 versus the same 2024 period, and September 2025 alone spiked 283% year-over-year5556. Statutory damages run $500-$1,500 per violation with no aggregate cap56. Real-estate exposure is concrete: Realogy/Coldwell Banker settled a DNC class action for $20M in January 2025; Keller Williams paid $40M in 2023; Fathom Realty agreed to $2.85M on a text-message matter57. State “mini-TCPA” laws add a parallel burden, with at least 15 states enacting their own statutes by late 202558.
  3. The carve-out that protects REDX’s core. Agents may manually dial homeowners about expired listings for up to 18 months after expiration even when the number is on the DNC registry, and FSBO and expired manual calls are broadly permitted because manual, non-autodialer calling falls outside the autodialer restrictions59. Phone prospecting of expired and FSBO leads remains legal in 2025 when done manually. The honest frame for the buyer is that the rule got easier while the lawsuits got more frequent, so the value is a vendor whose data and dialing keep the agent on the right side of DNC and manual-dialing lines.
  4. Which competitors stack which attestations. Mojo publishes the most explicit compliance stack: STIR/SHAKEN Attestation A registration, spam-tag monitoring, and automated appeals through its Reputation Guard36. Vulcan7 markets Tier-1 carrier spam protection. Espresso markets whitelisted numbers across all packages49. Compliance content marketing is itself table stakes; REDX, ArchAgent, and CompliancePoint all publish DNC/TCPA guides60.
  5. Auditable-specifics checklist. A punch list, not a vibe. What the buyer in this category looks for, and where REDX stands:
Attestation the ICP looks for REDX status
DNC scrubbing / DNC flagging on delivered lists Shipped (REDX scrubs/flags DNC)12
Whitelisted / branded caller numbers [client to confirm]
STIR/SHAKEN Attestation A registration [client to confirm]
Spam-tag monitoring and remediation [client to confirm]
Published DNC/TCPA agent guidance Shipped (REDX blog DNC/TCPA guide)60
Manual-dial compliance support for expired/FSBO 18-month window [client to confirm]

7. Vulnerability Watch

These are the exposures a REDX rep can use head-to-head. The live-page forensic catches lead because they are the highest-value and the hardest for a competitor to explain away.

Vulcan7 ships a broken placeholder link on its primary conversion page. The Vulnerability. A credibility artifact on the page where buyers convert. The Evidence. Vulcan7’s /sales/ lead-capture form contains a broken placeholder hyperlink rendered as “about:blank” shipped to production61. The Window. Open, and it closes the moment Vulcan7 reviews its own funnel. Use it now. Recommended Response. When a prospect cites Vulcan7’s premium polish, note that the page where Vulcan7 asks for your money ships a broken link, and ask what that says about the data behind it.

Vulcan7 states two different data-superiority numbers on two live pages. The Vulnerability. The “#1 data accuracy” claim contradicts itself. The Evidence. The homepage says “14% more unique numbers per listing than any other provider”; the packages FAQ says “20% more unique numbers”13. The same page set also states “over 60,000 agents” on the homepage and “over 35,000 agents” in the FAQ, a 25,000-agent gap62. The Window. Open until Vulcan7 reconciles its own copy. Recommended Response. Ask the prospect which Vulcan7 number is true, the 14% or the 20%, and point out that a company that cannot keep its own headline straight is selling precision it does not practice.

Landvoice’s contract marketing is contradicted by its own refund response. The Vulnerability. “No long-term contracts” is not what buyers experience. The Evidence. Landvoice markets “no long-term contracts” and a simple cancellation policy, yet told a BBB reviewer “our policy does not allow for refunds after the commitment is made,” and multiple reviewers report one-year annual-billed contracts with refunds denied; its BBB customer review average is 1/54314. The Window. Open and durable, because it is documented in the public BBB record. Recommended Response. Read the prospect Landvoice’s own words back: the site says no contracts, the refund desk says no refunds after commitment. Offer REDX’s published monthly price as the verifiable alternative.

Espresso Agent shipped its site with a competitor’s template labels and zeroed counters. The Vulnerability. The “industry-leading” scale story is undercut by the site itself. The Evidence. Espresso’s navigation still carries unremoved third-party template labels from an unrelated product, “Stiddle Connect,” “Stiddle Identify,” “Stiddle Visualize,” “Stiddle Pixel Attribution,” and “Stiddle Workspaces”16. Its About page stat counters render as literal zeros: “0 Agents Served Since 2014,” “0 Million+ Leads Delivered To Agents”15. The Window. Open until Espresso cleans its template and fixes the counters. Recommended Response. When a prospect weighs Espresso on scale, point them to Espresso’s own About page, where the agents-served counter reads zero and a competitor’s product names are still pasted in the menu.

Mojo runs a maintenance modal inside its primary sales flow, with vintage copy a year stale. The Vulnerability. The page that should close sales tells buyers to come back later, and the site looks unmaintained. The Evidence. Mojo’s pricing page (the “Build Your Mojo Plan” conversion flow) carries a live modal reading “Maintenance in Progress. We are currently undergoing technical maintenance. Please try again in one hour”63. Every page footer reads “© 2026” while page titles still say 2025 (“Build Your Real Estate Dialer Plan in 2025”), and the CMS theme version differs across pages64. The Window. Open until Mojo republishes; the maintenance modal is the kind of artifact that recurs on an unevenly maintained site. Recommended Response. If a prospect is mid-evaluation of Mojo, ask whether they saw the maintenance notice on the pricing page, and note that a dialer whose own checkout is “under maintenance” is a risky place to run a daily calling business.

Mojo’s call-quality hero claim is contradicted by its own worst reviews. The Vulnerability. “The Call Quality Leader” is the exact thing customers complain about. The Evidence. Mojo brands itself “The Call Quality Leader,” yet its Trustpilot rating is “Poor” at 2.3/5 with complaints of “terrible audio quality,” and a G2 reviewer reports the dialer “hangs up on customers mid sentence”3438. The Window. Open while the reviews stand. Recommended Response. When a prospect repeats Mojo’s call-quality pitch, note that Mojo’s own Trustpilot reviews rate it “Poor” on the audio its marketing leads with.

Zillow’s lead economics are weak even before the litigation. The Vulnerability. High cost, low conversion, non-exclusive leads. The Evidence. Zillow Premier Agent leads convert at 1-3% (under 2% in some luxury markets) and are sold to 1-4 agents at once151. A new class action filed November 2025 (Armstrong v. Zillow) alleges Premier Agents must hit Zillow Home Loans pre-approval quotas as a condition of access to high-value leads, an allegation, not an adjudicated fact65. The Window. Open and widening as costs rise and litigation continues. Recommended Response. Walk a Zillow-spending prospect through the math: a $300-500 monthly spend (far more in metro ZIPs) for leads sold to several agents that close at 1-3%, versus a must-transact expired owner at a fraction of the cost66.

8. Wallet-Share Watch: Zillow and BombBomb

These two compete for the agent’s marketing dollar across data, video, and portal advertising. The rep’s job against them is the cost-per-outcome and intent argument.

Zillow. Zillow is the dominant top-of-funnel portal, 221 million monthly users in Q4 2025, roughly double its nearest rival, which is the real source of its pricing power over agents67. That power buys agents low-intent volume. Premier Agent spend runs $300-500/month outside metros and $1,000-plus inside them, with cost-per-lead from $20 up to $450 in hot ZIPs, for leads sold to as many as four agents that close at 1-3%66151. Agents describe the leads as “renters or people looking for the listing agent” and “browsers with no intent to buy”68. A class action filed November 2025 alleges lead access is conditioned on Zillow Home Loans pre-approval quotas; this remains an allegation awaiting adjudication65. REDX’s counter is the must-transact seller: an expired or pre-foreclosure owner who has to move, at a fraction of Zillow’s cost, with a pipeline the agent owns outright.

BombBomb. BombBomb is a video-messaging tool, now repositioned as a horizontal “video engagement platform” across five industries with real estate just one of them, priced per user at $36-$70/month6970. It provides no prospect data, no skip-tracing, no lead lists, and no dialer, so it competes only for share of the agent’s communication budget71. It runs across email, text, and LinkedIn, so the literal “phone-only” framing describes a different rival; the honest distinction here is a full system against a single comms feature72. Its annual contracts are non-cancellable, with a documented auto-renewal complaint73. The REDX position is to fold video into the end-to-end prospecting system (data, dialer, multi-channel follow-up including video) so the agent gets the channel as one piece of a prospecting engine rather than a standalone $36-$70 video line item.

9. 7 Powers Reads

The durable question is which advantages survive a determined challenger and which are temporary leads or press-release framing.

  1. Vulcan7. Branding is the one real power, a fifteen-year premium reputation that reviewers reinforce with “Bentley” framing, and it is soft31. It rests on perception, so a credible data-parity demonstration deflates it. The data-spend claim (“11x more”) is a buy advantage any capitalized rival can match30. The 12-month no-refund contract is a manufactured switching cost that punishes the leaver28. No network effect, no decisive scale lead.
  2. Mojo. Its one genuine, narrow edge is the copper-based, non-VoIP call path, a real technical differentiator competitors cannot trivially copy35. Branding from a 2007 name is modest. With no contracts, switching costs are weak. There is no data or platform moat.
  3. Landvoice. Its claimed power is counter-positioning, three ranked numbers against REDX’s volume model, which is a real philosophical fork but not a structural moat41. Brand and “over 30 years” tenure are reputational. Being data-only with no dialer or CRM lock-in leaves the lowest switching cost in the set4.
  4. Espresso Agent. The only defensible power is counter-positioning on price, an all-in bundle undercutting Vulcan7, plus soft switching costs from its coaching community2522. Scale and cornered-resource claims are unsupported; its own broken counters undercut the scale story15.
  5. Zillow. The one durable power in this whole report sits here: a genuine network and scale advantage from 221M monthly users that no competitor in REDX’s category approaches67. That moat protects Zillow’s pricing power, not its lead quality, which is exactly where REDX attacks.
  6. BombBomb. A modest, real brand in video messaging and moderate switching costs from accumulated content and CRM embeds69. It claims a “patented fallback video logic,” the one IP asset surfaced, with the patent specifics unverified. No scale or network moat.

10. Strategic Implications

REDX matches every direct rival on data and beats them on system breadth, so lead the brand with the complete system plus the coaching that turns a willing agent’s work into listings, proven by named peers, and demote the “best data” headline. The proof library, 16 named agents with hard numbers, is the asset no rival publishes5.

REDX’s own customers file the same accuracy complaints that sink Mojo and Landvoice, so fix the data-hygiene experience (de-dupe DNC, disconnected, and already-listed numbers) before any “best data” claim, and never lead on accuracy until the fix ships912.

Every direct rival is phone-first or data-only, so promote REDX’s multi-channel orchestration (call, text, email, video, ads) from a feature list into the headline identity against the single-line-dialer field243.

Vulcan7 hides prices and traps buyers in a 12-month no-refund term, so publish REDX’s prices openly and operationalize a genuine, buyer-verifiable monthly cancellation as a head-to-head trust weapon2328.

Coaching alone is not the moat because Vulcan7 and Espresso coach too, so anchor the coaching claim to the published named-agent proof library and keep building that library20225.

Zillow’s buyer leads convert at 1-3% and are sold to multiple agents, so arm reps with the must-transact seller-lead intent argument and the cost-per-outcome math against the portal model for every wallet-share conversation151.

The new and part-time work-willing agent is priced out of the team stack and underserved, so target that segment with the guided system-plus-coaching identity rather than competing on raw-data price with Mojo and Landvoice50.

REDX leadership’s belief that Espresso is a Vulcan7 sub-brand is contradicted by public record, so retire that claim from any external messaging and treat Espresso as an independent competitor unless private ownership evidence is confirmed4648.

Position BombBomb’s video as a feature inside REDX’s end-to-end system rather than a standalone spend, so reps reframe a $36-$70 video line item as one channel of the full prospecting cadence REDX already runs71.

A note on MyTrustLeads

“MyTrustLeads” was named once in a REDX leadership interview and cannot be confirmed to exist as a real-estate prospecting vendor under any spelling tested74. In the same interview, when asked directly for his three main competitors, the speaker named only Landvoice, Vulcan7, and Mojo and did not repeat it, which points to a misspoken or conflated name75. The closest real match is “my +plus leads” (myplusleads.com), an Austin-based expired/FSBO vendor76. No profile, features, pricing, or positioning is attributed to “MyTrustLeads” because none could be verified. Treat it as not a tracked competitor.

11. Battle Cards

11.1 Vulcan7

30-second positioning summary. Vulcan7 is the fifteen-year premium incumbent. Its data is good, its brand is strong, and its price is the highest in the class at roughly $359/month behind hidden pricing and a 12-month no-refund contract2628. REDX matches the data inside a broader multi-channel system at roughly a sixth of the entry price, with the proof and coaching that move a willing agent to listings.

Top 3 attack lines (rep can say).

  • “Vulcan7 won’t show you a price until you talk to sales, then locks you into a year. We publish ours.”
  • “Their homepage says 14% better data; their own FAQ says 20%. Which number are you buying?”
  • “Vulcan7 is a single-line dialer with video email. REDX runs call, text, email, video, and ads from one place.”

Top 3 defense lines (rep can say).

  • (If the prospect says Vulcan7 has better data:) “Vulcan7 buys more data, and a buy advantage is one anyone can match. Their own customers report calling for three months and getting zero, wrong numbers, and homes already sold33. Run both lists for a week and judge the connects, not the marketing.”
  • (If the prospect says Vulcan7 is the trusted premium brand:) “That premium is reputation, the ‘Bentley’ framing coaches repeat, not a better outcome31. You are paying about $359 a month for fifteen-year-old positioning. REDX puts the same-grade data in a fuller system for a published price.”
  • (If the prospect says Vulcan7 offers cancel-anytime:) “Their site says cancel anytime; their reviewers describe a pushed 12-month no-refund term28. REDX’s monthly price is one you can confirm before you sign.”

Key proof points (publicly verifiable).

  • Vulcan7 publishes no prices on its packages page; every CTA routes to a sales form23.
  • Its /sales/ page ships a broken “about:blank” placeholder link61.
  • A documented customer paid “more than $900” over three months for “the answer is 0”33.

Case math for this deal.

  • Proof point to read aloud (vertical-matched): “Len Nevin was a part-time agent. He quit his job and now makes $250,000 prospecting expired leads with REDX”77.
  • TCO worksheet (rep fills live): Vulcan7 published price = ____ (none shown; ask the prospect what sales quoted). Contract term = ____ months. Middleware sync add-on = ____/mo. REDX entry = ~$59/mo, monthly. Connects per hour on a one-week head-to-head = ____ (Vulcan7) vs ____ (REDX).

When the prospect asks about Vulcan7. “Vulcan7 is the established premium name and the data is solid. The catch is what it costs and how they sell it: no public price, a sales call, and a year-long no-refund contract for around $359 a month, on a single-line dialer2628. Their own pages can’t agree whether their data edge is 14% or 20%13. If you want the same grade of data inside a system that also texts, emails, and runs ads, at a price you can see today, that’s REDX.”

11.2 Mojo Sells

30-second positioning summary. Mojo is a fast dialer with a real call-quality story and a low headline price that climbs fast once you stack the modules27. It sells tools and coaches no one21. REDX gives a willing agent the data, the multi-channel system, and the guided coaching Mojo leaves out.

Top 3 attack lines (rep can say).

  • “Mojo looks cheap until you add it up: $149 a month before a single lead, then $250-plus with data27.”
  • “Mojo’s loudest complaint is dead data, agents reaching two people in three hundred calls37.”
  • “Mojo sells you a dialer and walks away. There’s no academy, no coaching, no roadmap21.”

Top 3 defense lines (rep can say).

  • (If the prospect says Mojo’s dialer is faster and cheaper:) “The speed is real; the price isn’t as low as it looks. Required Agent Access plus the Triple Line is $149 before data, and real configs run past $25027. REDX gives you the dialer plus follow-up channels Mojo doesn’t, for a published entry near $5924.”
  • (If the prospect says Mojo has the best call quality:) “Mojo brands itself ‘The Call Quality Leader,’ yet its Trustpilot is ‘Poor’ at 2.3 with ‘terrible audio quality’ and a dialer that ‘hangs up mid sentence’38. Test the audio yourself before you trust the slogan.”
  • (If the prospect says Mojo’s data is fine:) “Capterra calls Mojo’s data ‘extremely inaccurate,’ with agents reaching two people in three hundred calls37. REDX’s data has its own accuracy work to do, and we’re honest about it, but you also get coaching and multi-channel follow-up Mojo never offers.”

Key proof points (publicly verifiable).

  • Mojo’s pricing floor is $149/month before any data17.
  • Its pricing page carried a live “Maintenance in Progress” modal in the conversion flow63.
  • Its About page contains no coaching, academy, or guided onboarding21.

Case math for this deal.

  • Proof point to read aloud: “Kent Brown made $177,000 in 53 days making about 1,000 calls a day with REDX”78.
  • TCO worksheet (rep fills live): Mojo = $10 Agent Access + $139 Triple Line + Expired $50 + FSBO $25 + Skip Tracer $49 = ____/mo. REDX entry = ~$59/mo plus the modules the agent actually needs = ____/mo. Coaching included? Mojo: no. REDX: yes.

When the prospect asks about Mojo. “Mojo is a genuinely fast dialer and the copper-based call path is a real engineering choice35. Two things to weigh: the price isn’t as low as the headline once you stack the dialer and data past $250, and Mojo coaches no one, it hands you a tool and you’re on your own2721. REDX gives you a dialer plus text, email, and video follow-up, and the coaching and named-agent proof that show a willing agent how to turn calls into listings.”

11.3 Landvoice

30-second positioning summary. Landvoice is a curated seller-data house with no dialer and a founder who helped start REDX442. Its “no long-term contracts” pitch is contradicted by its own refund-denial record and a 1/5 BBB review average14. REDX adds the outbound dialer Landvoice lacks and the honest billing its customers say it broke.

Top 3 attack lines (rep can say).

  • “Landvoice has no outbound dialer at all, just an inbound capture number4.”
  • “Their site says no contracts; their refund desk says no refunds after the commitment4314.”
  • “Their BBB customer reviews average one star out of five, mostly on billing14.”

Top 3 defense lines (rep can say).

  • (If the prospect says Landvoice’s curated 3-number data is higher quality:) “That’s a real philosophy, three ranked numbers instead of every number41. The tradeoff is volume: a high-activity prospector runs out of contacts. REDX gives you the depth to keep dialing all morning, plus the dialer to do it.”
  • (If the prospect says Landvoice has no contracts:) “That’s the marketing. A BBB reviewer was told ‘our policy does not allow for refunds after the commitment is made,’ and several report being held to a billed year14. REDX’s monthly price is one you can verify before you commit.”
  • (If the prospect trusts Landvoice because its CEO co-founded REDX:) “True, and it shows how close the data is42. The difference is the system: Landvoice is data only, no dialer, no multi-channel4. REDX is the full prospecting engine around the data.”

Key proof points (publicly verifiable).

  • Landvoice offers only inbound Call Capture, no outbound dialer4.
  • It told a BBB reviewer refunds are not allowed after commitment, against its “no contracts” copy14.
  • Its BBB customer review average is 1/5 and it is not BBB accredited14.

Case math for this deal.

  • Proof point to read aloud: “Steve Matthews was a struggling agent and generated $300,000 in commissions prospecting FSBOs with REDX”7.
  • TCO worksheet (rep fills live): Landvoice bundle = ____/mo (Starter $87 to Elite $197), billed ____ (monthly/annual). Dialer included? No, add a separate dialer = ____/mo. REDX entry ~$59/mo with the dialer built in.

When the prospect asks about Landvoice. “Landvoice does one thing, curated seller data, and the founder connection to REDX tells you the data is close42. Two gaps matter: there’s no dialer, so you’ll buy calling separately, and the ‘no contracts’ promise is contradicted by their own BBB refund record414. REDX gives you the data, the dialer, and multi-channel follow-up in one place, with billing you can actually verify.”

11.4 Espresso Agent

30-second positioning summary. Espresso is the affordable feature-clone of Vulcan7, phone-only by choice, with a website leaking a competitor’s template labels and zeroed counters31615. REDX matches the bundle and the coaching, adds true multi-channel reach, and ships a credible surface.

Top 3 attack lines (rep can say).

  • “Espresso’s dialer page says it on purpose: one call at a time, no texting, no automation3.”
  • “Their About page counters read zero, ‘0 Agents Served Since 2014’15.”
  • “Their menu still has another company’s product names pasted in, the ‘Stiddle’ labels16.”

Top 3 defense lines (rep can say).

  • (If the prospect says Espresso is cheaper and does the same thing:) “It’s close on the bundle, and it’s phone-only by design, one call at a time, no texting3. REDX adds the channels Espresso refuses, so the same willing agent reaches more owners more ways for a published entry near $5924.”
  • (If the prospect says Espresso’s data is accurate in reviews:) “Their data reputation is decent, and their own copy hedges it with ‘where available’45. REDX’s edge isn’t a data brag, it’s the multi-channel system and the named-agent proof around the data.”
  • (If the prospect believes Espresso is owned by Vulcan7 and therefore enterprise-grade:) “Public records say otherwise: Espresso is The Symphony Source in American Fork, Utah; Vulcan7 is a separate company in Cincinnati4648. Same look, different companies, and Espresso’s own site ships a competitor’s template labels16.”

Key proof points (publicly verifiable).

  • Espresso’s dialer page states “One call at a time” and “No auto-texting or pre-recorded voicemails”3.
  • Its About page stat counters render as literal zeros15.
  • Its navigation carries unrelated “Stiddle” template labels16.

Case math for this deal.

  • Proof point to read aloud: “Emily Peckham sold more than 160 FSBOs in five years with REDX”8.
  • TCO worksheet (rep fills live): Espresso = $199 to $399/mo (Pro $279), term plan notice window = 15 days25. REDX entry ~$59/mo, monthly, multi-channel included. Channels: Espresso phone-only; REDX call + text + email + video + ads.

When the prospect asks about Espresso. “Espresso is the affordable Vulcan7 look-alike, and its data reviews are decent45. The honest limits: it’s phone-only by design, one call at a time with no texting, and the site itself looks unfinished, with zeroed counters and another product’s labels still in the menu31615. If you want the affordable bundle but with real multi-channel reach and a polished system, that’s REDX.”

11.5 Zillow (wallet-share)

30-second positioning summary. Zillow is the traffic giant, not a data-dialer rival, and it sells agents expensive, non-exclusive buyer leads that convert at 1-3%151. REDX competes for the same marketing dollar with must-transact seller leads at a fraction of the cost, owned by the agent.

Top 3 attack lines (rep can say).

  • “Zillow sells the same inquiry to up to four agents; you’re bidding against your own competitors for it51.”
  • “Those leads close at one to three percent, often under two in luxury1.”
  • “Spend starts at $300-500 a month and runs past $1,000 in metros, with no cap66.”

Top 3 defense lines (rep can say).

  • (If the prospect says Zillow has the most buyers:) “It does, and most of them aren’t ready, renters, browsers, people who want the listing agent68. An expired or pre-foreclosure owner has to move. That’s the difference between traffic and intent.”
  • (If the prospect says Zillow leads are warm because they’re live-connected:) “Agents who pay for that still report leads that never answer and were never serious68. You’re renting access to a shared pool; with REDX the pipeline is yours.”
  • (If the prospect says Zillow Flex costs nothing upfront:) “Flex takes up to 40% of your commission at close and you can’t negotiate it79. A REDX seller lead costs a flat, published monthly price and keeps your whole commission.”

Key proof points (publicly verifiable).

  • Zillow leads convert at 1-3% and are sold to 1-4 agents151.
  • Premier Agent spend runs $300-500/mo non-metro and $1,000-plus metro66.
  • Zillow Flex success fees run 15-40% of commission, raised to 40% in six test markets, non-negotiable79.

Case math for this deal.

  • Proof point to read aloud: “Kayla Dieppa grew to $14M in volume in a year on REDX expired and FSBO leads, about 40% year-over-year”5.
  • TCO worksheet (rep fills live): Zillow monthly spend = ____ (ask the agent); cost per closed deal at 2% conversion = ____. REDX entry ~$59/mo; seller leads owned, not shared.

When the prospect asks about Zillow. “Zillow has the traffic, no question. What you’re buying is volume, not intent: leads sold to several agents at once, closing at one to three percent, for a spend that climbs into the thousands in a metro51166. REDX’s seller leads are owners who have to transact, expireds, FSBOs, pre-foreclosures, at a flat published price you own outright. Put the cost-per-closed-deal side by side and the seller list wins.”

  • Paula Burlison earns $522,000 with the Power Dialer, REDX case study (https://www.redx.com/case-study/prospector-discovers-power-dialer-earns-522000-listing-homes/).

  • 160+ FSBO listings, Emily Peckham, REDX case study (https://www.redx.com/case-study/how-mastering-fsbos-with-redx-led-to-160-listings/).

  • BBB REDX LLC complaints, Shalece L. and others (https://www.bbb.org/us/ut/orem/profile/sales-lead-generation/redx-llc-1166-22014712/complaints).
  • BBB REDX LLC complaints, disconnected/DNC numbers (https://www.bbb.org/us/ut/orem/profile/sales-lead-generation/redx-llc-1166-22014712/complaints).
  • Customer-voice synthesis across G2 (https://www.g2.com/products/redx/reviews), BBB (https://www.bbb.org/us/ut/orem/profile/sales-lead-generation/redx-llc-1166-22014712/complaints), Trustindex/Google (https://www.trustindex.io/reviews/theredx.com), Birdeye Vulcan7, and Software Finder Landvoice.

  • BBB Landvoice Data LLC customer reviews, Draper UT (https://www.bbb.org/us/ut/draper/profile/sales-lead-generation/landvoice-data-llc-1166-22168844/customer-reviews).

  • Mojo Sells homepage (https://www.mojosells.com/).
  • Landvoice Data Genie page (https://www.landvoice.com/data-genie).
  • Vulcan7 homepage, training (https://www.vulcan7.com/).
  • Mojo Sells About page (https://www.mojosells.com/about/).
  • Espresso Agent Launch page (https://www.espressoagent.com/launch).
  • Vulcan7 packages page (https://www.vulcan7.com/packages/).
  • Landvoice pricing page (https://www.landvoice.com/pricing).
  • Espresso Agent pricing page (https://www.espressoagent.com/pricing).
  • RealEstateSkills, Vulcan7 reviews (https://www.realestateskills.com/blog/vulcan7-reviews).
  • PowerDialer.ai, Mojo Dialer Pricing 2025 (https://www.powerdialer.ai/blog/mojo-dialer-pricing-2025); REsimpli (https://resimpli.com/blog/mojo-dialer-pricing/).
  • RealEstateSkills, Vulcan7 reviews, contract terms (https://www.realestateskills.com/blog/vulcan7-reviews).
  • G2 REDX reviews, data-accuracy complaints (https://www.g2.com/products/redx/reviews).
  • Vulcan7 homepage, data claims (https://www.vulcan7.com/).
  • RealEstateSkills, Vulcan7 reviews, premium-brand framing (https://www.realestateskills.com/blog/vulcan7-reviews).
  • Birdeye, Vulcan7 Real Estate Leads aggregate (https://reviews.birdeye.com/vulcan7-real-estate-leads-157971466939289).
  • Birdeye, Vulcan7 Real Estate Leads, Eduardo F. (https://reviews.birdeye.com/vulcan7-real-estate-leads-157971466939289).
  • Mojo Sells homepage and power-dialer page (https://www.mojosells.com/ ; https://www.mojosells.com/power-dialer/).
  • Mojo Sells power-dialer page (https://www.mojosells.com/power-dialer/).
  • Mojo Reputation Guard and Caller ID pages (https://www.mojosells.com/mojo-reputation-guard/ ; https://www.mojosells.com/caller-id/).
  • Capterra Mojo reviews (https://www.capterra.com/p/207288/Mojo/reviews/); AI and Realtors (https://aiandrealtors.com/review-mojo-dialer); PowerDialer.ai.
  • G2 Mojo Dialer reviews (https://www.g2.com/products/mojo-dialer/reviews); Trustpilot mojosells.com (https://www.trustpilot.com/review/mojosells.com).
  • Landvoice homepage (https://www.landvoice.com/).
  • Landvoice pricing page, Expired Pro (https://www.landvoice.com/pricing).
  • Landvoice pricing page, REDX comparison FAQ (https://www.landvoice.com/pricing).
  • Landvoice pricing page, founder statement (https://www.landvoice.com/pricing).
  • Landvoice pricing page, contract and setup-fee copy (https://www.landvoice.com/pricing).
  • BBB Landvoice Data LLC customer reviews, cancellation complaints (https://www.bbb.org/us/ut/draper/profile/sales-lead-generation/landvoice-data-llc-1166-22168844/customer-reviews).
  • Espresso Agent homepage (https://www.espressoagent.com/).
  • Espresso Agent EULA / The Symphony Source LLC subscription agreement (https://www.espressoagentapp.com/usercontent/by_name/espresso/backoffice/policies/Espresso_EULA.pdf).
  • Espresso Agent About page, founding story (https://www.espressoagent.com/about-us).
  • ZoomInfo, Vulcan7 (https://www.zoominfo.com/c/vulcan-7/356502219).
  • Espresso Agent pricing page, bundle contents (https://www.espressoagent.com/pricing).
  • LA Metro Home Finder / Allied Schools team-vs-solo analyses, 2025-2026 (https://www.lametrohomefinder.com/blog/should-new-agents-join-real-estate-team-or-go-solo-expert-guide); agent-attrition corroboration across coaching sources.
  • The Close, Zillow Premier Agent cost (https://theclose.com/zillow-premier-agent-worth-cost/).
  • Birdeye, Vulcan7 Real Estate Leads, Zac M. (https://reviews.birdeye.com/vulcan7-real-estate-leads-157971466939289).
  • Software Finder, Landvoice reviews, Josiah K. and Barbara S. (https://softwarefinder.com/marketing-software/landvoice/reviews).

  • National Law Review, midyear/September 2025 TCPA reports (https://natlawreview.com/article/midyear-litigation-report-tcpa-class-actions-staggering-952-2024-previously-highest); TCPAWorld, Mar 6 2025 (https://tcpaworld.com/2025/03/06/tcpa-class-action-filings-explode-the-massive-final-numbers-are-in-for-2024-and-january-2025-numbers-are-insane/).
  • National Law Review (https://natlawreview.com/article/midyear-litigation-report-tcpa-class-actions-staggering-952-2024-previously-highest); TCPAWorld midyear (https://tcpaworld.com/2025/07/25/midyear-litigation-report-tcpa-class-actions-up-staggering-95-2-from-2024-previously-the-highest-year-on-record/); National Law Review Sept 2025 (https://natlawreview.com/article/woah-tcpa-class-actions-just-spiked-283-september-2025-and-out-control).
  • Law360 / National Mortgage Professional, Jan 2025 (https://nationalmortgageprofessional.com/news/realogy-settles-tcpa-class-action-lawsuit-20m); Top Class Actions (https://topclassactions.com/lawsuit-settlements/closed-settlements/20m-coldwell-banker-real-estate-calls-class-action/); InjuryClaims, Fathom Realty (https://injuryclaims.com/class-action-lawsuits/class-action-privacy-data-and-tcpa/fathom-realty-tcpa-lawsuit-settlement).
